summary: Wat Tham Krabok refugee camp in Saraburi, Thailand was where my family lived before coming to the United States in 2004. It was a place of refuge and hope for us, especially for my younger siblings and me who were born and raised there. My father carried his qeej with him from this camp all the way to Saint Paul, Minnesota. Wat Tham Krabok was truly home to us during those years before our migration.

Saraburi Province in Thailand is where the Wat Tham Krabok refugee camp is located, the place where my family stayed from 1993 until we moved to the United States in 2004. It was in Saraburi that my younger siblings and I were born and raised. This province became a temporary home for us during a difficult time in our lives as refugees. It holds many memories of our life before starting anew in America.

Saint Paul, Minnesota is where my family and I arrived on September 16, 2004, after leaving the refugee camp in Thailand. It marked the beginning of our new life in the United States. I remember the journey vividly, especially my father carrying his qeej carefully wrapped in black cloth. Saint Paul became our new home where we hoped for better opportunities and a fresh start.

The United States is the country my family immigrated to from Wat Tham Krabok refugee camp in 2004. It represented hope and a chance for a better life for us. My father brought his qeej all the way here, showing how important his culture is even in a new land. Living in the U.S. has been a journey of preserving our heritage while adapting to a new world.

My father, Vang Thao, was born in 1955 in Vang Vieng, Laos. He moved with his family to Vientiane in 1979 where he got married, and later to Wat Tham Krabok refugee camp in Thailand in 1993. He is a respected qeej player who learned the instrument from his father starting at age five. Despite not having formal education, his skill with the qeej earned him respect in our community. He carefully carried his qeej with him all the way to the United States in 2004, showing how much it means to him.

Vang Vieng, Laos is where my father, Vang Thao, was born in 1955. It is his hometown and the place where his roots and early life began. The qeej he carries with him reminds him of Vang Vieng and who he is as a person. Although we left Laos many years ago, Vang Vieng remains an important part of our family history.

Laos is where my father's journey began; he was born in Vang Vieng and later moved to Vientiane in 1979. It is the country of our heritage and the place where our family lived before becoming refugees. Our migration story includes moving from Laos to Thailand and eventually to the United States. Laos is deeply connected to our identity and traditions, especially through the qeej and our Hmong culture.

Vientiane, Laos is where my father moved with his family in 1979 and where he got married. It was an important chapter in his life before we became refugees. This city represents a part of our family's past and the life they had before the upheaval that led us to Thailand and eventually the United States. Vientiane holds memories of family and beginnings for my father.

The qeej is a traditional Hmong reed instrument that my father has played since he was five years old. He learned it from his dad and eventually bought his own at age 30 for 3,000 baht. The qeej is very important in our culture, played at funerals to honor the dead and during the Hmong New Year celebrations. It’s a difficult instrument to learn because it involves both musical and spoken parts. My father’s qeej traveled with us from the refugee camp in Thailand all the way to the United States, symbolizing our heritage and identity.

The Hmong people are my community and culture, deeply connected to the traditions my father has passed down, especially through the qeej. In our society, those who could play traditional instruments like the qeej were highly respected, even if they didn’t have formal education. Our customs, such as playing the qeej at funerals and the Hmong New Year, keep our heritage alive. Being Hmong means embracing these traditions and remembering where we come from, even as we live far from Laos.

The Hmong New Year is a special event where the qeej is played to celebrate the upcoming year. It is a time of cultural pride and community gathering for the Hmong people. My father has played the qeej during these celebrations, connecting us to our heritage. Even after moving to the United States, the Hmong New Year remains an important occasion to honor our traditions and identity.
